Tweet by @trevin (Trevin Chow)
Author bio: Obsessing over AI and building with it. Open source incl. @ppressdev @illo_skill and Compound Engineering.
Text: https://t.co/LLxcji2q4C
Created: 2026-06-27T03:32:28.000Z
Metrics: {"retweet_count":15,"reply_count":12,"like_count":146,"quote_count":3,"bookmark_count":329,"impression_count":36020}
Article content: {"entities":{"urls":[{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/blob/main/docs/skills/ce-code-review.md"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/blob/main/docs/skills/ce-polish.md"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/blob/main/docs/skills/ce-brainstorm.md"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin#installation"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/blob/main/docs/skills/ce-ideate.md"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/blob/main/docs/skills/ce-setup.md"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/blob/main/docs/skills/ce-brainstorm.md"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/blob/main/docs/skills/ce-brainstorm.md"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/blob/main/docs/skills/ce-plan.md"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/blob/main/docs/skills/ce-plan.md"},{"text":"https://github.com/EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin/blob/main/docs/skills/ce-plan.md"}]},"plain_text":"The version may not indicate it, but this is a big release for the plugin. The architecture is different now, the docs are shaped differently, agents run longer and better, and we’re fully embracing /goal.
We killed our own agents (on purpose)
This is potentially the biggest structural change since the plugin first launched.
Previously, Compound Engineering relied on dedicated standalone agent definitions. Those worked great in Claude Code. They worked less fine – or didn't work – across Codex, Cursor, Gemini, Pi, and OpenCode, because formal agent definitions aren't a reliable common denominator across harnesses. Every harness does agents slightly differently. That meant we were either maintaining harness-specific copies or leaving users on something that partially worked and required manual out-of-band updates to stay current.
We removed all of that and essentially tore things down to the studs.
Every skill is now self-contained. We still achieve our specialist “agent” behavior, but everything lives in skill-local prompt assets vs formal agent definition. It’s super detailed, potentially too nuanced, but it’s impact will be huge for existing and new users of the plugin.  No copying scripts. No nested paths. No "you need to re-run setup after updating."
The practical effects:
Codex CLI and Codex desktop app – works cleanly now, including as a full-fledged plugin with auto-updates. Before this, Codex desktop required workarounds.
OpenCode, Pi and Cursor – much better native support. The plugin being skills-only means it can slot into plugin systems that don't have a concept of standalone agents at all.
The plan is now “/goal” ready
We used to produce two documents: a requirements doc and an implementation plan which is echo from our flawed all-human-worker ways. They served different stages of the workflow, which made sense conceptually. The problem in practice is that requirements change as implementation goes on. When they do, you have two documents to update, they can drift, you have to remember which one is authoritative on a given question, and agents have to hold both in context and reconcile them.
The new unified “plan” document collapses these into a single artifact. When you run /ce-brainstorm it’ll first lay out the scenarios and the requirements. If you follow with /ce-plan, the document gets updated to also cover the implementation plan details. So you have one complete doc package for you and your agent.
The more important change is what that document is designed to do. It's “/goal” ready: it has a clear definition of done, the scope is bounded, and the implementation approach is explicit enough that an agent can operate from it without needing to check in. At the end of /ce-plan, we offer an option to just start a /goal feeding in the plan so you can then just walk away (or switch to another agent for another set of work!).  The agent knows what a complete picture of what done looks like because it's in the document.
In testing on complex features, that loop has held up well. I’ve had several multi-hour runs on complete features, with one topping 6 hours.  The agent implemented the features fully, wrote tests, opened a PR, and shipped it with no human in the loop beyond the initial plan handoff. On features with UI, at the end of the initial set of agent work, there’s room for you to review, iterate and changes things and where our skills like /ce-polish comes in.
Portability is a bonus too: one document is easier to pass between agents, attach to a task, or drop into a new context.
Documents that draw, when drawing helps
Plans and ideas aren't always best as walls of prose. Some things want a diagram.
So the skills now support real HTML output as an option, and when you use it the document supplements the prose with visuals — diagrams where a diagram lands the point better than a paragraph. ce-ideate leans into this and outputs HTML by default, because ideation is inherently a human-in-the-loop moment and a human reads a sketched idea faster than a spec. The unified plan document supports the same treatment.
A few things to know about how to drive it:
Markdown is still the default. Most Compound Engineering usage — including ours — is feeding documents straight to agents, and markdown is the right call there. HTML is opt-in.
Try it in one line. Add output=html or "use html format" the next time you run ce-brainstorm or ce-plan.
Set it once and forget it. Put your preferred default format in your AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md or your Compound Engineering config so you're not retyping it every prompt.
It respects your taste. If your repo has a DESIGN.md at the root, the skills use it to influence the document's design while keeping it legible.
For most users – including us – markdown stays the default. Most of the time the document is going straight to an agent, and agents don't need HTML. The visual path is there for the cases where a human is reading and the content benefits from it.
A few more things
Cross-model adversarial review. ce-code-review gained a cross-model adversarial pass — a second model actively trying to break the first one's work. We also right-sized the review and routed it through one portable path so it behaves consistently everywhere.
Smarter PR-feedback resolution. ce-resolve-pr-feedback now judges findings centrally before dispatching fixers, instead of firing off fixes in parallel and hoping.
ce-brainstorm got eyes. Visual feedback during brainstorming instead of just text change.  When a discussion calls for it, the agent will spin up a small visual artifact to get your input and preview it in a small web server, then tear the session down when you’re done.  Even works in Codex app.
Marketplace + install, fixed across the board. Aligned plugin and marketplace metadata, 50% reduction in skill descriptions, and installation is easier and faster on more harnesses like Pi and OpenCode.
How to get it
This release includes a marketplace update, which means your harness's normal update mechanism may not be enough to pick it up. The safest path for existing installs is to remove the plugin and reinstall it fresh from the marketplace. That's a bit annoying, but it's the move that guarantees you're on the right version with the right plugin data. We also fixed some marketplace and plugin data issues that had been causing friction on first-time installs, so the process should be cleaner than it's been.
To reinstall: remove Compound Engineering from your harness, then follow the installation steps in the repo README. Installation varies by harness, so the README is the right reference for your specific setup.
If you're new: run ce-setup in any project after installing and it'll get you going. The goal-ready plan doc and HTML output are there to try immediately.
Why this one matters
Most releases add things, but this one feels much bigger for some reason. Update your plugin. Run /ce-brainstorm on something real. Let us know how it goes!","preview_text":"The version may not indicate it, but this is a big release for the plugin. The architecture is different now, the docs are shaped differently, agents run longer and better, and we’re fully embracing","cover_media":"3_2070710773266128896","title":"Compound Engineering Update – June 26, 2026"}
